content_markdown:
The variation with potential difference $V$ of the current $I$ in an electric lamp is shown below.

At point P, the current is $I_p$, the potential difference is $V_p$ and the gradient of the tangent to the curve is $G$. What is the resistance of the lamp at point P?

diagram_metadata.description: A graph showing current I on the y-axis and potential difference V on the x-axis. The curve starts at the origin and curves downwards (decreasing gradient). A point P is marked on the curve with corresponding coordinates (Vp, Ip).

vibe_explanation: Resistance is defined by Ohm's Law as the ratio of potential difference to current at a specific point ($R = V/I$). While the gradient $G$ of an $I-V$ graph relates to the dynamic conductance, the actual resistance at point P is simply the ratio of the voltage coordinate to the current coordinate at that point.
